source.title: Machine Learning Systems, Volume 1 - Chapter 5: AI Workflow
source.license_note: Open textbook page; summarized as system-design lessons for InfRing.

source_lessons.summary: Later-stage monitoring, deployment, and evaluation evidence must inform earlier design, data, model, workflow, and runtime decisions.
AI systems are iterative and empirical; treating lifecycle stages as independent or final creates hidden production failures.
Validation belongs throughout the lifecycle, not only at the end; skipping validation creates production-only failure modes.
Development metrics can fail to predict production behavior because production has data quality, latency, resource, and adversarial constraints.
Data quality, runtime constraints, deployment realities, and monitoring feedback influence each other; local optimization is insufficient.

infring_assimilation_dimensions.required_behavior: Runtime evidence must classify workload shape, freshness, volume, latency pressure, source coverage, and production-like constraints before confidence is promoted.
Low-confidence or stale evidence routes to bounded probes, worksheets, or validation runs instead of implementation or issue auto-promotion.
Default paths must preserve explicit payload, runtime, and report-size budgets so monitoring/feedback loops do not become new failure sources.
Findings should follow Diagnose -> Act -> Monitor: identify root-cause hypothesis, route a concrete action, then attach follow-up monitoring/proof before closure.

guardrails: Do not treat one clean run as production readiness.
Do not promote development-only metrics without production-like workload evidence.
Do not skip validation to accelerate implementation.
Do not let monitoring outputs grow without report-size budgets.
Do not route low-confidence observations into closed-loop mutation.
